intro_band.agent_remit_summary: A natural-language interface to a relational database: it converts a user's question into a SQL query, runs it against a pre-configured SQLAlchemy connection, and returns a natural-language answer based on the results. The agent may use exactly four tools — sql_db_list_tables, sql_db_schema, sql_db_query_checker, and sql_db_query — and is expected to limit result sets to top_k rows (default 10), never SELECT *, run the query-checker before executing, answer "I don't know" for off-topic questions, and stay within max_iterations (default 15). DML, DDL, administrative/superuser commands, multi-statement queries, and queries outside the configured table scope are forbidden entirely — there is no approval path — and the remit explicitly states that if the agent code is the sole enforcement point for the DML/DDL prohibition, that is itself a finding.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: A LangChain library component: create_sql_agent() in agent_toolkits/sql/base.py wires an LLM plus a SQLDatabaseToolkit (four tools defined in tools/sql_database/tool.py) into a langchain_classic AgentExecutor — the docstring itself flags this executor as legacy and not actively supported for new production apps. Domain and behavioral constraints live entirely in the SQL_PREFIX system prompt (agent_toolkits/sql/prompt.py); there is no code-level SQL parsing, statement-type check, row-limit injection, or table-scope check at execution time. QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run passes the LLM-generated SQL string straight to SQLDatabase.run_no_throw → _execute in utilities/sql_database.py, which runs it inside a committing engine.begin() transaction; the user's question is concatenated into the prompt as {input} with no validation, and table names, schema, sample rows, and query results are returned into the LLM context unsanitized and unlabeled.

behavior_summary:
The dominant pattern is policy declared in the system prompt with essentially zero code-level enforcement. Every behavioral constraint the remit cares about — no DML/DDL/admin statements, no SELECT *, the top_k row cap, run-the-checker-first, the configured table scope, "answer 'I don't know' when off-topic" — exists only as English in SQL_PREFIX. QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run hands the model's SQL string directly to SQLDatabase._execute, which runs it in a committing transaction with no statement inspection, no row limit, and no table-scope check; the sql_db_query_checker "gate" is itself just another LLM call that reproduces the query.

Layered on top is a clean injection chain (PRAX-002): the user's question goes into the prompt as {input} unvalidated, and table names / schema / sample rows / query results come back into the LLM context unsanitized — so either a crafted question or instruction text hidden in a row reaches the SQL-generation step, and from there into arbitrary execution against the database in one hop. The remit explicitly anticipates this and says the agent code must not be the sole enforcement point for the DML/DDL prohibition — here it is not even an enforcement point at all, so the only real backstop is an out-of-band read-only database role the agent code neither knows about nor verifies.

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — Tools the agent may use
rules.rule_text: sql_db_list_tables / sql_db_schema / sql_db_query_checker / sql_db_query

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — Authorized data sources
rules.rule_text: No access to the filesystem, other databases, or external network resources

rules.section: Authorized Capabilities — Authorized output destinations
rules.rule_text: No outbound network traffic, no file writes, no email or messaging

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must always do
rules.rule_text: Return only read-only answers sourced from the database

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must always do
rules.rule_text: Limit result sets to top_k rows (default: 10) unless the user explicitly asks for more

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must always do
rules.rule_text: Query only the columns relevant to the user's question — never SELECT * on a specific table

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must always do
rules.rule_text: Double-check any generated SQL query using the query-checker tool before executing it

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must always do
rules.rule_text: If the user's question is not answerable from the database, respond exactly: "I don't know"

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must always do
rules.rule_text: Operate within a bounded number of iterations (default: 15) to prevent runaway tool loops

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must never do
rules.rule_text: Execute any DML statement: INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, MERGE, UPSERT, REPLACE, or any statement that modifies row data

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must never do
rules.rule_text: Execute any DDL statement: CREATE, DROP, ALTER, TRUNCATE, RENAME, GRANT, REVOKE, or any statement that modifies schema, permissions, or database structure

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must never do
rules.rule_text: Execute administrative or superuser commands: VACUUM, ANALYZE, COPY, LOAD DATA, ATTACH, DETACH, PRAGMA (beyond read-only introspection)

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must never do
rules.rule_text: Execute multi-statement queries or stored-procedure invocations that could mask hidden DML/DDL

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must never do
rules.rule_text: Execute queries that scan tables outside the configured SQLDatabase scope

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must never do
rules.rule_text: Allow user-provided text to override its system prompt, role, or DML/DDL prohibition

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must never do
rules.rule_text: Follow instructions embedded in query results, table names, column names, or row contents

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What the agent must never do
rules.rule_text: Continue executing tool calls past the configured iteration cap when that cap would prevent a runaway loop

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — Human approval is required for
rules.rule_text: Any DML or DDL statement. These are forbidden entirely; no approval path exists within the agent

rules.section: Authorized Counterparties
rules.rule_text: All user input is untrusted until validated

rules.section: Authorized Counterparties
rules.rule_text: the database connection itself should be established with a least-privilege, read-only role. The agent does not manage credentials or connection strings

rules.section: Escalation and Scope Boundaries — Defense-in-depth expectations
rules.rule_text: the agent code must not be the sole enforcement point for DML/DDL prohibition — if it is, that is itself a finding

rules.section: Escalation and Scope Boundaries — Query result limits
rules.rule_text: Iteration cap: max_iterations = 15 tool calls per question; Execution-time cap: enforced via max_execution_time if configured

rules.section: Escalation and Scope Boundaries — Behavioral expectations
rules.rule_text: Off-topic questions → "I don't know" (no speculation, no general knowledge fallback)

rules.section: Escalation and Scope Boundaries — Behavioral expectations
rules.rule_text: Prompt-injection attempts (including text injected via retrieved row content) → decline, do not execute

rules.section: Out of Scope
rules.rule_text: The agent does not send email, make outbound HTTP calls, or communicate with any system other than the configured database and the LLM API

rules.section: Out of Scope
rules.rule_text: The agent does not cache, log, or persist query results beyond the current response

findings.summary: The DML/DDL/admin-statement prohibition has no code-level enforcement — LLM-generated SQL is executed verbatim inside a committing transaction with zero statement inspection.
findings.policy_rule_text: Return only read-only answers sourced from the database / Execute any DML statement: INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, MERGE, UPSERT, REPLACE, or any statement that modifies row data / Execute any DDL statement: CREATE, DROP, ALTER, TRUNCATE, RENAME, GRANT, REVOKE... / Execute administrative or superuser commands: VACUUM, ANALYZE, COPY, LOAD DATA, ATTACH, DETACH, PRAGMA... / Execute multi-statement queries or stored-procedure invocations that could mask hidden DML/DDL / Any DML or DDL statement. These are forbidden entirely; no approval path exists within the agent / the agent code must not be the sole enforcement point for DML/DDL prohibition — if it is, that is itself a finding
evidence: tools/sql_database/tool.py:53-59 — QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run(query) calls self.db.run_no_throw(query) with no inspection of the query string
utilities/sql_database.py:621-647 — run_no_throw → run → _execute; no DML/DDL/admin/multi-statement check anywhere in this path

utilities/sql_database.py:475-552 — _execute wraps the model-supplied command in `with self._engine.begin() as connection:` (a transaction that commits) and runs `connection.execute(text(command), ...)` — any INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE/DROP/ALTER the model emits is committed

agent_toolkits/sql/prompt.py:12 — the only DML restriction is the prose line `DO NOT make any DML statements (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, DROP etc.)` in SQL_PREFIX; DDL, admin commands, and multi-statement queries are not mentioned even there
Absence: no SQL parser, statement-type allow-list, read-only execution mode, or per-statement policy exists in any staged file
findings.recommended_action: Do not rely on the system prompt or the LLM query-checker for the DML/DDL prohibition. Add a deterministic pre-execution check in QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run (or in SQLDatabase.run) that parses the statement (e.g., with sqlglot/sqlparse), rejects anything that is not a single SELECT (or other explicitly-allowlisted read statement), and rejects multi-statement input; pair it with a read-only execution path (read-only transaction / autocommit-off + rollback) so the committing engine.begin() block in _execute can never persist a write. Treat the operator's read-only DB role (R-20) as the second layer, not the only one.

findings.summary: Compound injection chain: the user's question enters the prompt as {input} unvalidated and tool outputs (table names, schema, sample rows, query results) re-enter the LLM context unsanitized, so a crafted question or instruction text hidden in a row reaches SQL generation and then arbitrary execution in one hop.
findings.policy_rule_text: Allow user-provided text to override its system prompt, role, or DML/DDL prohibition / Follow instructions embedded in query results, table names, column names, or row contents / All user input is untrusted until validated / Prompt-injection attempts (including text injected via retrieved row content) → decline, do not execute
evidence: agent_toolkits/sql/base.py:221,237 — `HumanMessagePromptTemplate.from_template("{input}")`: the user question is templated straight into the prompt with no sanitization or instruction/content separation

tools/sql_database/tool.py:114-120 — ListSQLDatabaseTool._run returns raw table names; tool.py:92-100 — InfoSQLDatabaseTool._run returns `get_table_info_no_throw(...)` (raw CREATE TABLE text + sample rows); tool.py:53-59 — QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run returns raw `run_no_throw` output — all flow back into the LLM context as tool messages with no labeling as untrusted
utilities/sql_database.py:445-473 — _get_sample_rows pulls live row data into the schema string handed to the model

Absence: no input validation, no content-origin tagging, and no prompt-injection heuristic anywhere in the staged code; combined with PRAX-2026-05-11-001 (no statement inspection on execution) the chain runs to a committing SQL transaction
findings.recommended_action: Treat both the user question and every tool output as untrusted: keep them in clearly-delimited, role-separated message slots (already partly the case) and add the deterministic statement check from PRAX-2026-05-11-001 so that even a hijacked SQL-generation step cannot produce a write or out-of-scope read. For sample rows / schema returned by InfoSQLDatabaseTool, consider truncating or omitting free-text cell contents that are not needed for query planning.

findings.summary: The query-checker is advisory only — nothing requires sql_db_query_checker to run before sql_db_query, and the checker is itself just an LLM prompt that reproduces the query and checks for syntax mistakes, not for DML/DDL.
findings.policy_rule_text: Double-check any generated SQL query using the query-checker tool before executing it
evidence: tools/sql_database/tool.py:53-59 — QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run has no parameter or state recording whether the checker was invoked; it executes whatever string it is given
agent_toolkits/sql/toolkit.py:119-126 and tools/sql_database/prompt.py:135-138 — the 'always use this tool before executing' instruction lives only in the tool description / SQL_PREFIX prose

tools/sql_database/prompt.py:2-18 — the QUERY_CHECKER prompt only flags syntax issues (NOT IN with NULL, UNION vs UNION ALL, identifier quoting, etc.) and is told to 'reproduce the original query' if there are no mistakes — it never checks statement type
tools/sql_database/tool.py:161-171 — QuerySQLCheckerTool._run just calls `self.llm_chain.predict(...)`; its output is not bound to the subsequent sql_db_query call
findings.recommended_action: Stop treating the LLM query-checker as a control. Make the deterministic statement check from PRAX-2026-05-11-001 the mandatory pre-execution gate inside QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run so it cannot be skipped, and either drop the implication that sql_db_query_checker is a safety gate or have QuerySQLDatabaseTool require evidence the query was checked.

findings.summary: The top_k row cap is interpolated into the system prompt only — no LIMIT is applied in code, so a runaway or injected query can return an unbounded result set.
findings.policy_rule_text: Limit result sets to top_k rows (default: 10) unless the user explicitly asks for more
evidence: agent_toolkits/sql/base.py:175 — `prefix = prefix.format(dialect=toolkit.dialect, top_k=top_k)`: top_k is only substituted into the SQL_PREFIX prose ('always limit your query to at most {top_k} results')
utilities/sql_database.py:568-603 — run() / _execute() apply no row cap to model-supplied queries; `fetchall()` returns every row (only schema sample rows use `.limit()`, at sql_database.py:447)
tools/sql_database/tool.py:53-59 — QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run passes the query straight through with no LIMIT enforcement
findings.recommended_action: Enforce the row cap server-side of the LLM: in SQLDatabase.run (or QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run) wrap or rewrite the statement to apply a hard LIMIT / fetch ceiling derived from top_k (e.g., fetchmany(top_k) plus a 'results truncated' note), independent of whatever the model wrote.

findings.summary: The 'never SELECT * on a specific table' rule is prompt-only — no code prevents the agent from selecting all columns.
findings.policy_rule_text: Query only the columns relevant to the user's question — never SELECT * on a specific table
evidence: agent_toolkits/sql/prompt.py:7 — the only restriction is the prose line `Never query for all the columns from a specific table, only ask for the relevant columns given the question` in SQL_PREFIX
tools/sql_database/tool.py:53-59 and utilities/sql_database.py:475-566 — the query path executes whatever the model emits, including `SELECT *`, with no column-scope check
findings.recommended_action: If the no-SELECT * rule must be enforced (it is largely a data-minimization concern), add it to the deterministic statement check from PRAX-2026-05-11-001 — reject star projections against base tables — rather than relying on the prompt; otherwise downgrade it in the remit to a best-effort prompt guideline.

findings.summary: The configured-table scope only filters the introspection tools — sql_db_query executes arbitrary SQL against the engine, so the agent can read tables placed in ignore_tables or outside include_tables.
findings.policy_rule_text: Execute queries that scan tables outside the configured SQLDatabase scope
evidence: utilities/sql_database.py:333-337 — get_usable_table_names() applies include_tables/ignore_tables; this is consumed only by ListSQLDatabaseTool (tool.py:120) and get_table_info (tool.py:98, sql_database.py:361-365) for what is *shown* to the model
utilities/sql_database.py:475-552 — _execute runs `connection.execute(text(command), ...)` with no check that the referenced tables are within the usable set
tools/sql_database/tool.py:53-59 — QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run does no table-scope validation on the query string
findings.recommended_action: Make table scope a property of execution, not just of introspection: in the deterministic statement check from PRAX-2026-05-11-001, resolve the tables referenced by the parsed query and reject any not in get_usable_table_names(); back it with a DB role / schema scoping (R-20) so the agent connection cannot reach out-of-scope tables even if the parser is bypassed.

findings.summary: The 'answer exactly "I don't know" for off-topic / unanswerable questions, no speculation' behavior is enforced only by the system prompt and can be talked out of via injection.
findings.policy_rule_text: If the user's question is not answerable from the database, respond exactly: "I don't know" / Off-topic questions → "I don't know" (no speculation, no general knowledge fallback)
evidence: agent_toolkits/sql/prompt.py:9,14 — `Only use the information returned by the below tools to construct your final answer` and `If the question does not seem related to the database, just return "I don't know" as the answer` are the only places this rule appears; both are in SQL_PREFIX
Absence: no code path inspects the final answer for grounding or off-topic content; nothing forces the literal `"I don't know"` string
findings.recommended_action: This is a soft behavioral rule that genuinely lives in the prompt; mark it accordingly in the remit and, if stronger assurance is needed, add a lightweight post-generation check that the answer references at least one tool result before returning it (otherwise emit the canonical "I don't know").

findings.summary: The extra_tools parameter lets create_sql_agent attach arbitrary additional tools — including send/exec/write capabilities — beyond the four-tool Known Good Baseline, with no allow-list or review gate.
findings.policy_rule_text: No access to the filesystem, other databases, or external network resources
evidence: agent_toolkits/sql/base.py:63 — `extra_tools: Sequence[BaseTool] = ()` and base.py:171 — `tools = toolkit.get_tools() + list(extra_tools)`: any caller-supplied tools are added to the agent's inventory unconditionally
agent_toolkits/sql/base.py:46-68 — create_sql_agent also accepts an arbitrary `prompt`, `prefix`, and `**kwargs`, so the system prompt and agent class can be replaced wholesale by the caller
Absence: no allow-list, capability classification, or warning when extra_tools introduces fs/net/write/exec capability
findings.recommended_action: Default extra_tools is empty, so the risk is realized only by the integrating application — document in create_sql_agent that adding tools, replacing the prompt, or overriding kwargs takes the agent outside the SQL-only baseline, and have integrators pin the toolkit to the four tools unless a reviewed exception exists.

findings.summary: max_iterations defaults to 15 (matching the remit) but is Optional[int] — a caller can pass None, disabling the runaway-tool-loop cap entirely.
findings.policy_rule_text: Operate within a bounded number of iterations (default: 15) to prevent runaway tool loops / Continue executing tool calls past the configured iteration cap when that cap would prevent a runaway loop
evidence: agent_toolkits/sql/base.py:58 — `max_iterations: Optional[int] = 15`; base.py:266 — passed through to AgentExecutor, which enforces it when set
agent_toolkits/sql/base.py:58 — the `Optional` type means a caller can pass `max_iterations=None`, removing the cap; there is also no max_execution_time default (see PRAX-2026-05-11-010)
findings.recommended_action: Keep the default at 15 but consider clamping or warning when a caller disables it (e.g., refuse None, or log a warning), so the runaway-loop cap cannot be silently removed by the integrating application.

findings.summary: max_execution_time defaults to None — no execution-time cap is enforced by default, and the docstring itself notes client-side timeouts may not cancel a running statement on the database server.
findings.policy_rule_text: Iteration cap: max_iterations = 15 tool calls per question; Execution-time cap: enforced via max_execution_time if configured
evidence: agent_toolkits/sql/base.py:59 — `max_execution_time: Optional[float] = None` — no time budget unless the caller sets one

agent_toolkits/sql/base.py:96-97 (docstring) — 'Client-side timeouts do not always guarantee that a running statement is cancelled on the database server'; the code provides no DB-side statement timeout
utilities/sql_database.py:475-552 — _execute passes no timeout / statement_timeout to the connection
findings.recommended_action: Document that operators must set a server-side statement timeout on the connection (e.g., statement_timeout for PostgreSQL) and pass max_execution_time; consider a non-None default for max_execution_time so an unconfigured deployment still has a wall-clock bound.

findings.summary: No action-level logging — generated SQL, tool invocations, and results are never recorded; AgentExecutor.verbose prints to stdout only and is off by default, so the no-enforcement gaps above are also undetectable.
findings.policy_rule_text: the agent code must not be the sole enforcement point for DML/DDL prohibition — if it is, that is itself a finding
evidence: Absence: no `logging` calls in agent_toolkits/sql/base.py, agent_toolkits/sql/toolkit.py, tools/sql_database/tool.py, or utilities/sql_database.py for queries, tool calls, or results

agent_toolkits/sql/base.py:61,259-268 — the only observability hook is `verbose` passed to AgentExecutor (prints intermediate steps to stdout); it defaults to False and produces no durable, structured record
utilities/sql_database.py:621-647 — run_no_throw swallows SQLAlchemyError into a returned string with no log of the failed/attempted statement
findings.recommended_action: The remit deliberately scopes out result persistence (R-26), so do not log result rows — but do emit a structured, durable record of each generated SQL statement, the tool that ran it, success/failure, and row count, so the no-code-enforcement gaps (PRAX-2026-05-11-001, PRAX-2026-05-11-002) are at least detectable after the fact. A LangChain callback handler on the AgentExecutor is the natural seam.

positives.title: Tool inventory matches the remit's Known Good Baseline exactly
positives.description: SQLDatabaseToolkit.get_tools() returns precisely the four authorized tools — sql_db_query, sql_db_schema, sql_db_list_tables, sql_db_query_checker — and no more; the agent's default capability surface is tightly scoped.

positives.title: Prominent security warning and server-side-controls guidance in the public API
positives.description: create_sql_agent's docstring opens with a warning that the agent can execute arbitrary SQL, explicitly tells operators to use least-privilege read-only DB roles, statement timeouts and query guardrails, notes client-side timeouts may not cancel server statements, and points to Deep Agents for production — the maintainers are transparent about the known limitation.

positives.title: Runaway-loop cap on by default
positives.description: max_iterations defaults to 15 and is passed through to the AgentExecutor, which enforces it — a real circuit breaker against tool-call loops without any operator configuration.

positives.title: Result-cell truncation limits data volume in the LLM context
positives.description: SQLDatabase.run truncates each returned value to max_string_length (default 300 chars), and sample rows are clipped to 100 chars per cell — bounding how much raw data from any single field enters the prompt.

log_files.no_logs_note: No log files exist in the workspace and the agent code emits no action-level logs; the absence of any durable audit trail is filed as PRAX-2026-05-11-011 (the remit's R-26 deliberately scopes out result persistence, but not action-level logging).

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Ad hoc. The agent has a tightly-scoped, accurately-declared tool set and a system prompt that states the right constraints, plus a default iteration cap and a transparent security warning in its public API — but almost every behavioral and forbidden-action rule (no DML/DDL/admin statements, the top_k row cap, no SELECT *, run-the-checker-first, the configured table scope) is enforced only by that prompt, with no SQL parsing, statement-type allow-list, row limit, table-scope check, input validation, output filtering, or logging in code. Implement Zero Trust — the heaviest-weighted category — is the floor: the LLM's SQL string is executed verbatim inside a committing transaction, and the only real backstop the remit anticipates (a read-only DB role) is out of band and unverified by the agent. Supply Chain and Red Team are scored low-confidence because dependency manifests and test suites are outside the staged workspace; the deprecation notice on the legacy AgentExecutor is the only in-scope supply-chain signal.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: The default toolkit is exactly the four authorized tools (toolkit.py:95-132) and the system prompt scopes the agent to SQL Q&A, but domain enforcement is prompt-only — no code gate on what SQL runs or which columns it touches — and create_sql_agent's extra_tools/prompt/**kwargs parameters let an integrator widen the surface arbitrarily (base.py:46-68, base.py:171).

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: Data sources are scoped via include_tables/ignore_tables and the prompt tells the model to answer only from tool output, and result cells are truncated (sql_database.py:591), but table names, schema, and live sample rows re-enter the LLM context unsanitized and unlabeled (tool.py:92-120, sql_database.py:445-473) — a wide indirect-injection path the remit explicitly wants closed (R-16, R-24).

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: There is no code-level interposition on the agent's tool calls beyond pydantic type-checking — QuerySQLDatabaseTool._run hands the LLM's SQL string straight to SQLDatabase._execute, which runs it in a committing engine.begin() transaction with no statement-type check, no row limit, no table-scope check, and no logging (tool.py:53-59, sql_database.py:475-566); user input is templated in unvalidated and the LLM 'query-checker' is itself just another model call.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: Dependency manifests and lock files are outside the staged workspace so pinning/SBOM cannot be verified; the in-scope signals are mixed-to-positive — optional connectors (databricks, cnosdb, duckdb) are imported defensively, no credentials sit in workspace files, and create_sql_agent's docstring is candid that it returns a legacy, not-actively-supported langchain_classic AgentExecutor and points to Deep Agents instead (base.py:79-85).

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: No test files, red-team artifacts, injection-test fixtures, or evidence of adversarial-finding-driven design changes are present in the staged workspace; the prominent 'can execute arbitrary SQL' warning and server-side-controls guidance in base.py:71-98 show some security awareness but are not evidence of adversarial testing.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: No logging calls exist in any staged file for generated SQL, tool invocations, or results; the only observability hook is AgentExecutor's verbose flag, which prints to stdout, is off by default, and produces no durable structured record (base.py:61,259-268) — and run_no_throw swallows DB errors into a returned string without logging the attempted statement (sql_database.py:621-647).
